**Session 2022-23**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Supplementary written evidence submitted by the News Media Association (OSB68)**

**Briefing Paper on the Online Safety Bill**

The News Media Association (the " **NMA** ") is the voice of UK national, regional and local newspapers in all their print and digital forms - a £4 billion sector read by more than 46 million adults every month. Our members publish over 900 news media titles - from The Times, The Guardian, The Daily Telegraph and the Daily Mirror to the Manchester Evening News, Kent Messenger, and the Monmouthshire Beacon.

This paper supports and supplements the oral evidence provided by the NMA to the House of Commons Public Bill Committee on 26 May 2022.

1.**What** **is the Government’s i ntention?**

1.1.The Government’s intention is clear - content from recognised publishers (both news publishers and broadcasters) must sit firmly outside the regulatory scope of the Online Safety Bill (the " **Bill** "):

" *Stakeholders raised concerns during the consultation about how the legislation will impact journalistic content online and the importance of upholding media freedom.* ***Content published by a news publisher on its own site (****e.g. on a newspaper or broadcaster’s website) will not be in scope of the regulatory framework and user comments on that content will be exempted**.*
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Supplementary written evidence submitted by the News Media Association (OSB68)**

**Briefing Paper on the Online Safety Bill**

The News Media Association (the " **NMA** ") is the voice of UK national, regional and local newspapers in all their print and digital forms - a £4 billion sector read by more than 46 million adults every month. Our members publish over 900 news media titles - from The Times, The Guardian, The Daily Telegraph and the Daily Mirror to the Manchester Evening News, Kent Messenger, and the Monmouthshire Beacon.

This paper supports and supplements the oral evidence provided by the NMA to the House of Commons Public Bill Committee on 26 May 2022.

1.**What** **is the Government’s i ntention?**

1.1.The Government’s intention is clear - content from recognised publishers (both news publishers and broadcasters) must sit firmly outside the regulatory scope of the Online Safety Bill (the " **Bill** "):

" *Stakeholders raised concerns during the consultation about how the legislation will impact journalistic content online and the importance of upholding media freedom.* ***Content published by a news publisher on its own site (****e.g. on a newspaper or broadcaster’s website) will not be in scope of the regulatory framework and user comments on that content will be exempted**.*

chunks.text: how the legislation will impact journalistic content online and the importance of upholding media freedom.* ***Content published by a news publisher on its own site (****e.g. on a newspaper or broadcaster’s website) will not be in scope of the regulatory framework and user comments on that content will be exempted**.* *In order to* *protect media freedom, **legislation will include robust protections for journalistic content shared on in-scope services. The government is committed to defending the invaluable role of a free media and is clear that online safety measures must do this.** The government will continue to engage with a range of stakeholders to develop our proposals."* Online Harms White Paper (emphasis added). 1.2.Though yet to be reflected in the Bill, the Culture Secretary rightly intends to strengthen the current protections for recognised news publishers and, more broadly, journalistic content: *"Anything a journalist and recognised news outlet \[…\] wants to put on the internet should be there, if it’s legal, of course. And everything journalists write is. No responsible journalist posts terrorist content or anything that is illegal. What this bill does, if those algorithms and if those platforms remove something which a journalist or a news outlet puts online, they have to **notify that journalist** that they’re about to remove that content, they have to **say why they’re going to remove it**, and they have to give the journalist the **right to appeal**, and the **content remains online while that happens**."* Nadine Dorries on This Morning on 16 March 2022 (emphasis added). 2.**Why is this Important?**
chunks.display_text: *In order to* *protect media freedom, **legislation will include robust protections for journalistic content shared on in-scope services. The government is committed to defending the invaluable role of a free media and is clear that online safety measures must do this.** The government will continue to engage with a range of stakeholders to develop our proposals."* Online Harms White Paper (emphasis added). 1.2.Though yet to be reflected in the Bill, the Culture Secretary rightly intends to strengthen the current protections for recognised news publishers and, more broadly, journalistic content: *"Anything a journalist and recognised news outlet \[…\] wants to put on the internet should be there, if it’s legal, of course. And everything journalists write is. No responsible journalist posts terrorist content or anything that is illegal. What this bill does, if those algorithms and if those platforms remove something which a journalist or a news outlet puts online, they have to **notify that journalist** that they’re about to remove that content, they have to **say why they’re going to remove it**, and they have to give the journalist the **right to appeal**, and the **content remains online while that happens**."* Nadine Dorries on This Morning on 16 March 2022 (emphasis added). 2.**Why is this Important?**

chunks.text: they’re about to remove that content, they have to **say why they’re going to remove it**, and they have to give the journalist the **right to appeal**, and the **content remains online while that happens**."* Nadine Dorries on This Morning on 16 March 2022 (emphasis added). 2.**Why is this Important?** 2.1.As the Bill stands, there is every incentive (including large fines and criminal sanctions) for platforms to take down journalistic content either via blanket application of algorithms that may not distinguish journalistic or news publisher content or by human moderators who may be domiciled outside of the UK. They would face no repercussions for removal or downrating of content, and with scant redress for reinstatement of that content. The risk is significant given publishers address subjects of controversy as a matter of course. Reporting on contentious or ‘harmful’ content does not amount to a harm in itself, but is a recognised public good. 2.2.The current draft places the balance of decision making over editorial content with platforms. Publishers risk double \- regulat ion under the regime, as they are currently subject to a robust and complex system of law, regulation and editorial codes.In addition, UK citizens would be impacted by a paucity of reliable news and information, which is precisely what this Bill is seeking to address. 2.3.The Culture Secretary has rightly recognised this threat. Speaking about the risk to press freedom and big tech accountability she said:
chunks.display_text: 2.1.As the Bill stands, there is every incentive (including large fines and criminal sanctions) for platforms to take down journalistic content either via blanket application of algorithms that may not distinguish journalistic or news publisher content or by human moderators who may be domiciled outside of the UK. They would face no repercussions for removal or downrating of content, and with scant redress for reinstatement of that content. The risk is significant given publishers address subjects of controversy as a matter of course. Reporting on contentious or ‘harmful’ content does not amount to a harm in itself, but is a recognised public good. 2.2.The current draft places the balance of decision making over editorial content with platforms. Publishers risk double \- regulat ion under the regime, as they are currently subject to a robust and complex system of law, regulation and editorial codes.In addition, UK citizens would be impacted by a paucity of reliable news and information, which is precisely what this Bill is seeking to address. 2.3.The Culture Secretary has rightly recognised this threat. Speaking about the risk to press freedom and big tech accountability she said:

chunks.text: law, regulation and editorial codes.In addition, UK citizens would be impacted by a paucity of reliable news and information, which is precisely what this Bill is seeking to address. 2.3.The Culture Secretary has rightly recognised this threat. Speaking about the risk to press freedom and big tech accountability she said: " *platforms have all the powers in the world they want right now to remove any journalistic content they want to. They don’t have to respond to you, they don’t have to tell you why they’ve removed that content, and they don’t have to put it back up again if they do listen to your complaint*." Nadine Dorries on This Morning on 16 March 2022. 3.**What needs to change?** 3.1.The Bill, as introduced, intends to provide two forms of exemption for news publishers: (i) one for news publisher content from some of the duties of care, where that content appears on, or is shared via, in-scope services; and (ii) one for news publishers’ websites (including below the line comments) from the scope of the legislation generally. *News publisher websites* 3.2.Despite Government’s clear intention that news publisher sites remain out of scope of the Bill, the revised drafting still fails to expressly state that the limited functionality exemption (Schedule 1 Para 4) includes ‘recognised news publishers’. 3.3.This creates two issues: (i) without an express statement, news publishers face an unnecessary hurdle to rely on the very mechanism that has been designed to exempt their services, including below-the-line comments on such sites; and (ii) it discourages innovation for fear that new functionalities would bring news publisher sites within scope. The Government must correct this to provide security to news publisher websites. *News publisher content*
chunks.display_text: " *platforms have all the powers in the world they want right now to remove any journalistic content they want to. They don’t have to respond to you, they don’t have to tell you why they’ve removed that content, and they don’t have to put it back up again if they do listen to your complaint*." Nadine Dorries on This Morning on 16 March 2022. 3.**What needs to change?** 3.1.The Bill, as introduced, intends to provide two forms of exemption for news publishers: (i) one for news publisher content from some of the duties of care, where that content appears on, or is shared via, in-scope services; and (ii) one for news publishers’ websites (including below the line comments) from the scope of the legislation generally. *News publisher websites* 3.2.Despite Government’s clear intention that news publisher sites remain out of scope of the Bill, the revised drafting still fails to expressly state that the limited functionality exemption (Schedule 1 Para 4) includes ‘recognised news publishers’. 3.3.This creates two issues: (i) without an express statement, news publishers face an unnecessary hurdle to rely on the very mechanism that has been designed to exempt their services, including below-the-line comments on such sites; and (ii) it discourages innovation for fear that new functionalities would bring news publisher sites within scope. The Government must correct this to provide security to news publisher websites. *News publisher content*

chunks.text: on the very mechanism that has been designed to exempt their services, including below-the-line comments on such sites; and (ii) it discourages innovation for fear that new functionalities would bring news publisher sites within scope. The Government must correct this to provide security to news publisher websites. *News publisher content* 3.4.We are pleased with the Bill’s definitions of " *News Publisher Content* " in Clause 49 and " *Recognised News Publishers* " in Clause 50 of the Bill.N ews publisher content posted onto, or shared via, in-scope user-to-user services (i.e., social media platforms), *is* excluded from the safety duties imposed by the Bill. 3.5.The fundamental issue that risks undermining the news publisher exemption itself, however, is that the Bill does not contain a duty *not* to remove news publisher content from those services. Instead, the Bill creates significant incentives through high penalties to make platforms and search engines err on the side of caution whenever their algorithms encounter content that might put them at risk and to favour a zealous approach to moderate news publisher content.This risks undermining public access to news. 3.6.It is reassuring that alongside the publication of the revised Bill, the DCMS Press Release confirmed that " *Ministers will also continue to consider how to ensure platforms do not remove content from recognised media outlets.*" A conclusion that echoes the Joint Committee report: " *We recommend that the news publisher content exemption is strengthened to include a requirement that news publisher content should not be moderated, restricted or removed unless it is content the publication of which clearly constitutes a criminal offence, or which has been found to be unlawful by order of a court within the appropriate jurisdiction* ".
chunks.display_text: 3.4.We are pleased with the Bill’s definitions of " *News Publisher Content* " in Clause 49 and " *Recognised News Publishers* " in Clause 50 of the Bill.N ews publisher content posted onto, or shared via, in-scope user-to-user services (i.e., social media platforms), *is* excluded from the safety duties imposed by the Bill. 3.5.The fundamental issue that risks undermining the news publisher exemption itself, however, is that the Bill does not contain a duty *not* to remove news publisher content from those services. Instead, the Bill creates significant incentives through high penalties to make platforms and search engines err on the side of caution whenever their algorithms encounter content that might put them at risk and to favour a zealous approach to moderate news publisher content.This risks undermining public access to news. 3.6.It is reassuring that alongside the publication of the revised Bill, the DCMS Press Release confirmed that " *Ministers will also continue to consider how to ensure platforms do not remove content from recognised media outlets.*" A conclusion that echoes the Joint Committee report: " *We recommend that the news publisher content exemption is strengthened to include a requirement that news publisher content should not be moderated, restricted or removed unless it is content the publication of which clearly constitutes a criminal offence, or which has been found to be unlawful by order of a court within the appropriate jurisdiction* ".

chunks.text: content exemption is strengthened to include a requirement that news publisher content should not be moderated, restricted or removed unless it is content the publication of which clearly constitutes a criminal offence, or which has been found to be unlawful by order of a court within the appropriate jurisdiction* ". 3.7.The NMA has devised amendments to achiev e a positive duty on platforms not to improperly remove news publisher content. The NMA is reassured that the Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for DCMS has confirm ed that the Government intends to *"table an amendment to make sure journalistic content cannot be removed until a proper right of appeal has taken place**.*" We look forward to seeing the text of these amendments as a matter of urgency to ensure this Bill achieves its objectives to protect and uphold free dom of speech, after which we would be pleased to follow up with this Committee on this critical point. **Sayra Tekin** **Director of Legal** **News Media Association** ***7 June 2022***
chunks.display_text: 3.7.The NMA has devised amendments to achiev e a positive duty on platforms not to improperly remove news publisher content. The NMA is reassured that the Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for DCMS has confirm ed that the Government intends to *"table an amendment to make sure journalistic content cannot be removed until a proper right of appeal has taken place**.*" We look forward to seeing the text of these amendments as a matter of urgency to ensure this Bill achieves its objectives to protect and uphold free dom of speech, after which we would be pleased to follow up with this Committee on this critical point. **Sayra Tekin** **Director of Legal** **News Media Association** ***7 June 2022***
